title: Pulling a cylinder wall on the wheel
subtitle: Drawing clay upward from an opened floor to create an even-walled cylinder
excerpt: Pulling is the motion that raises clay from the floor of an opened mound into a wall. One hand works inside the hollow, one outside; together they compress and lift the clay upward in a slow coordinated movement. The cylinder is the base form behind every thrown piece: bowls, mugs, and vases all start here.
sourceNotes: Wheel pulling technique derived from first principles. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), pottery entry.

content.text: After opening, the clay sits in a low, wide hollow with a thick floor-to-rim section.
Pulling

draws that thick section upward and thins it into a wall. On 500 g of stoneware, two or three pulls should produce a cylinder roughly 100 mm tall with walls 6-8 mm thick. Each pull compresses the clay; too few pulls leaves walls uneven, too many pulls collapses the form.
Hand positions
1. Inside hand

Insert the fingers of the dominant hand into the hollow with fingertips touching the inner wall just above the floor. The fingertips apply outward pressure from inside the wall. Keep the elbow low and braced against the body.
2. Outside hand

Place the other hand flat against the outside wall, directly opposite the inside fingertips. The outside hand applies inward pressure that meets and balances the outward pressure from inside. For taller walls, a
knuckle pull
gives a more stable contact point.
The pull
3. Lift upward with both hands together
With the wheel spinning at medium speed, apply inward-outward
compression

between both hands and draw them upward slowly together; the rate of rise should take three to four seconds from base to rim. Move slower than feels natural. Fast pulls thin the clay unevenly and cause the rim to wobble.
4. Ease off at the rim

As both hands reach the top of the wall, reduce pressure and let the hands move apart slightly. Continuing to compress at the rim thins it to nothing. The rim should end up slightly thicker than the wall below it; this makes it easier to trim and re-shape later.
Repeating the pull

After the first pull, add a little water with the sponge and repeat from the base. Each pull starts from just above the floor, not from where the last pull ended. Starting mid-wall produces a bulge. Two or three pulls on 500 g of clay will typically produce a cylinder of 90-110 mm. Resist pulling more; each pull introduces water and softens the clay.
What it looks like when it is right

The finished cylinder stands vertical with a consistent wall thickness: 6-8 mm at the base graduating to 4-6 mm near the rim. The inner surface has horizontal throwing lines from the finger contact. The rim is level and slightly thicker than the wall below. The cylinder does not sway or vibrate when the wheel is spinning.

items.fix: Add less water each pull; limit to three pulls on 500 g; if the clay collapses, wire off the piece and start again
items.cause: Clay too wet from excess water, or too many pulls

items.fix: Steady the rim by cupping both hands gently around it while the wheel spins; use a sponge to re-wet and re-level
items.cause: Hands moved at different speeds during the pull, or clay was not fully centred

items.fix: Always start each pull from the lowest point just above the floor; the compression moves the clay upward, not sideways
items.cause: Each pull started from mid-wall rather than from just above the floor
